title: Object-Format Transition, Compatibility, and Disaster Defense · Mastery Check
summary: A maintainer review compares near-miss operations for object-format transition, compatibility, and disaster defense and requires the smallest safe Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense with topology and content evidence. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Explain SHA-1 and SHA-256 repository object formats, repository initialization choice, ID length, storage identity, and why algorithms are not a cosmetic display option.
Evaluate compatibility object formats, translation tables, protocol exchange, clone and fetch interoperability, tooling assumptions, and repository immutability limits.
Inventory scripts, parsers, databases, APIs, hooks, CI, hosting, and documentation for Git 3.0 breaking defaults, long IDs, and version-gated behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure
steps.instruction:
quiz case — Case G62 is a quiz in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for defend repository policy, topology, recovery, scale, trust, release identity, maintenance, migration, drills, residual risks, and owner handoff under changed constraints.

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Gita Production Repository Defense: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — Case G69 is a quiz in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs.
